coredata.dc:title: Broadband and dynamic afterglow photoluminescent carbon dots for time-resolved optical information coding and encryption

coredata.dc:description: Afterglow photoluminescent (PL) carbon dots (CDs)-based materials with tunable and time-dependent colors are highly desirable for their potential applications in high-level information encryption and anti-counterfeiting, but their construction remains a significant challenge. Herein, we report several broadband and dynamic afterglow PL CDs@BA using 2,2′-Dithiodibenzoic acid (DTSA) as the precursor of CDs and boric acid (BA) as the matrix. A facile thermal treatment significantly regulates the distribution among the cyan-green emission edge states and red emission surface states associated with the oxygen-containing groups of CDs@BA. The balanced contributions from different emissive states with distinct spectra, lifetimes on both nanoseconds and milliseconds scales, and relative proportions endow the CDs@BA with the ultrabroad and afterglow PL that cover almost the entire visible region. The broadband PL under light excitation mainly originates from the emissions of different singlet states, and the broadband afterglow PL is attributed to the combined delayed fluorescence (DF) of different singlet-triplet states. In particular, CDs@BA demonstrates an evident time-dependent afterglow color changing from tangerine to cyan-green, due to an enhanced contribution to the afterglow from the red DF component. Furthermore, these CDs@BA manifest promising potentials for white-light display, optical encryption, and time-resolved anti-counterfeiting applications. Our findings provide an effective strategy to construct broadband and dynamic afterglow CDs-based materials and are significant for understanding the related photophysical mechanisms.

head.ce:title: Broadband and dynamic afterglow photoluminescent carbon dots for time-resolved optical information coding and encryption

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Afterglow photoluminescent (PL) carbon dots (CDs)-based materials with tunable and time-dependent colors are highly desirable for their potential applications in high-level information encryption and anti-counterfeiting, but their construction remains a significant challenge. Herein, we report several broadband and dynamic afterglow PL CDs@BA using 2,2′-Dithiodibenzoic acid (DTSA) as the precursor of CDs and boric acid (BA) as the matrix. A facile thermal treatment significantly regulates the distribution among the cyan-green emission edge states and red emission surface states associated with the oxygen-containing groups of CDs@BA. The balanced contributions from different emissive states with distinct spectra, lifetimes on both nanoseconds and milliseconds scales, and relative proportions endow the CDs@BA with the ultrabroad and afterglow PL that cover almost the entire visible region. The broadband PL under light excitation mainly originates from the emissions of different singlet states, and the broadband afterglow PL is attributed to the combined delayed fluorescence (DF) of different singlet-triplet states. In particular, CDs@BA demonstrates an evident time-dependent afterglow color changing from tangerine to cyan-green, due to an enhanced contribution to the afterglow from the red DF component. Furthermore, these CDs@BA manifest promising potentials for white-light display, optical encryption, and time-resolved anti-counterfeiting applications. Our findings provide an effective strategy to construct broadband and dynamic afterglow CDs-based materials and are significant for understanding the related photophysical mechanisms.

Broadband and dynamic afterglow photoluminescent CDs@BA were synthesized for time-resolved cryptographic applications.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature afterglow emission materials have attracted extensive interest due to their prolonged photoluminescence (PL) and promising applications for encryption [1,2], anti-counterfeiting [3,4], and electroluminescence devices [5,6]. Compared to traditional afterglow emission, time-dependent phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence (DF) have intriguing advantages in the context of signal outputs for message encryption owing to the additional time dimension to facilitate multilevel encryption, dynamic data storage, and significant augment of encryption security [7,8]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop advanced afterglow luminescent materials that exhibit time-dependent color changes, enabling the encoding of information across temporal and spatial dimensions and thereby enhancing the complexity of encryption techniques.

Time-dependent afterglow emission platforms have been demonstrated in pure organic crystals or polymers. For example, Chen et al. observed the phosphorescence color variations from orange to cyan as the temperature decreases in dibenzofuran derivatives [9]. Dou et al. developed a universal strategy to construct an effectively colorful and time-dependent phosphorescence system by introducing luminophores into sodium alginate chains [10]. The unique semi-rigidified sodium alginate chains form stronger hydrogen bonding networks that are effective oxygen barriers, thus facilitating triplet-related emissions. Additionally, there have been also reported that some rare-earth materials [11,12] demonstrate time-dependent afterglow luminescence. However, the synthesis of organic afterglow materials is usually tedious and time-consuming, while the preparation of rare-earth materials has environmental and cost concerns, hindering their practical applications. Developing innovative time-dependent afterglow luminescent alternatives with a facile and eco-friendlier strategy is highly desirable for advanced security applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widespread attention owing to their tunable PL properties, minimal toxicity, good biocompatibility, and economical synthesis methods [13,14]. These advantages facilitate their potential applications in many fields such as optical technologies [15–17], energy conversion [18–20], and particularly in the promising realm of biomedical applications [21–24], due to their effective near-infrared absorption and emission [25] and improved photothermal conversion efficiency [26]. Concurrently, CDs have also been paid intensive interest in constructing metal-free afterglow materials across diverse fields of information security [27], optoelectronics [28] and bioimaging [29,30]. To date, studies have shown that numerous effective strategies have been proposed for achieving afterglow within CDs, with reports detailing the enhancement of structural rigidity [31,32], rational element doping [33–35], and the manipulation of engineered crystals [36]. However, current CDs-based afterglow materials still present several issues, including a tendency towards short-wavelength emissions (blue and green) and the limitation of producing a single static afterglow color. These significantly hamper their applications in high-level information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. Recently, several groups have made efforts and attempts to synthesize excitation-dependent afterglow and dynamically color-changing phosphorescent or delayed fluorescent CDs and have achieved some significant progress. For example, Tan and co-workers first reported novel CDs-based phosphorescent materials with afterglow color changing from orange to green within 1 s [37]. Li et al. realized dynamic afterglow color tuning from yellow-green to blue via activating the blue emission center of surface states [38]. Chen's group reported a universal approach for synthesizing CDs-based phosphorescent materials by co-melting unconventional organics with boric acid, enabling the dynamic afterglow color of CDs to be shifted from orange, through white, to cyan over 8 s by adjusting precursor ratios [39]. Shi et al. synthesized a series of CDs-based nanocomposites with dynamic phosphorescence changing from yellow to green, which was assigned to the synergistic impact of structural confinement and targeted structural defects [40]. Despite considerable advances in the preparations of dynamic afterglow luminescent CDs-based systems [41,42], developing multicolor and dynamic afterglow luminescent CDs within a single system remains a formidable challenge. Meanwhile, the afterglow color tuning of mostly reported CDs-based materials is predominantly constrained to a relatively narrow spectral region. Extending the spectral region of dynamic afterglow luminescent CDs-based materials is a critical issue that demands further exploration.

In this work, we synthesized a novel class of CDs@BA with an ultrabroad PL and dynamic afterglow emission through a facile thermal treatment using 2,2′-dithiodibenzoic acid (DTSA) as the precursor of CDs and boric acid as the matrix. The results indicate that the types and distribution of emissive surface/interface states are closely associated with the thermally induced oxidation of precursors. The increased contents of oxygen-containing functional groups of CDs@BA significantly extend the prompt emission and afterglow PL from cyan-green to the red region by increasing the reaction temperatures. Steady-state and time-resolved PL analyses indicate that the broadband PL under light excitation mainly originates from the fluorescence of different singlet states, and the broadband afterglow PL is ascribed to the DFs of different singlet-triplet states. Specifically, the as-synthesized CDs@BA at 200 °C demonstrate an ultrabroad PL spectrum with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) up to 225 nm, suggesting a potential application as the white-light display device. In addition to a broadband prompt PL, the CDs@BA prepared at 230 °C also manifests a broadband time-dependent afterglow coloring changing from tangerine to cyan-green, due to the activated additional red DF component with a sub-millisecond lifetime and its dominant contribution. Furthermore, the as-synthesized CDs@BA exhibit their promising applications for advanced information encryption and anti-counterfeiting, with significantly enhanced security and recognition capabilities in information protection.

2,2′-dithiodibenzoic acid (DTSA), orthoboric acid (BA) and 4-mercaptobenzoic acid were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals Corp. All the chemical agents were used without further purification. Ultrapure water was obtained with a commercial purification system and used throughout the work.

10 mg of DTSA and 3 g of BA were dispersed in 50 mL of ultrapure water in a 150 mL beaker, then covered with tin foil and pierced with a series of small holes. The mixture solutions were put into a heating oven at 180 °C, 200 °C, and 230 °C for 10 h. After the beakers of products were naturally cooled down to room temperature, lumpy carbon composites could be observed and then crushed into powder for further use. For convenience, the as-prepared CDs in the BA matrix were labeled as CDs@BA1, CDs@BA2, and CDs@BA3 according to their reaction temperatures. When 4-mercaptobenzoic acid as the precursor was used, the synthesis was conducted at 180 °C for 10 h and the final product was labeled as CDs@BA0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs@BA composites for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: The composite materials were exposed to a UV lamp, and photographs were captured using a digital camera at different intervals. Thereafter, hollow-out anti-counterfeiting patterns (including a butterfly, Tai Chi diagram, and university emblem) and codes were superimposed on the photographs to retrieve corresponding information. The three circles in the code corresponded to CDs@BA3, CDs@BA2, and CDs@BA1 from left to right. The graceful ‘butterfly’ was associated with CDs@BA2, the iconic Tai Chi diagram was paired with both CDs@BA2 and CDs@BA1, and the distinguished emblem of Henan University is linked to CDs@BA3.

To eliminate the interference of impurities on imaging, the CDs solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove high molecular weight impurities. The remaining solution was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da for 24 h. Subsequently, prepared CDs@BA solution (20 μL) was dropped on an ultrathin carbon film-coated copper grid and contacted for 30 min, then the excess liquid was removed by filter paper and dried overnight in the oven before imaging. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained in an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 electron microscopy operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. For the atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis, the prepared CDs solutions (10 μL) were deposited onto a freshly cleaved muscovite mica surface by drop-casting and incubating for 10 min. After that, the un-adsorbed CDs were washed away gently with ultrapure water and blow-dried with nitrogen gas. The surface morphology of CDs was discerned using AFM (Slover P47H, NT-MDT) in tapping mode with NSG 11 tips (silicon cantilever).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on a PerkinElmer spectrometer through the potassium bromide method. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of samples was obtained on an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) by placing the sample powder on glass holders. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of samples were carried out on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (Thermo Fisher Nexsa), and the obtained data were further analyzed by using XPS Peak software.

Sample powders were dispersed on the surface of pure barium sulfate whiteboard. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained on a Cary 5000 UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Agilent) equipped with an integrating sphere. The emission spectra and lifetimes on the nanosecond scale were measured on an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrophotometer using a sub-nanosecond pulsed laser as the excitation source. The measurements of PL spectra and time-resolved decay profiles of the subsecond time scale were carried out on an FLS980 spectrometer equipped with a xenon arc lamp and a microsecond flashlamp. The obtained dynamic traces were fitted with a multi-exponential equation (1) combined with the deconvolution of the instrument response function.

Where I(t) is the luminescence dynamics signal with time at a selected wavelength, τ i represents the time constant of the decay component, and A i represents the corresponding relative amplitude of the component.

To understand the thermal effects on the photophysical properties of the as-synthesized CDs@BA, we performed a series of structural measurements and analyses. The morphologies of CDs@BA were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The results are shown in Fig. 2(b–d) for CDs@BA3, and Figs. S1–S2 for CDs@BA1 and CDs@BA2, respectively. The TEM images of CDs@BA3 indicate the formation of monodispersed CDs with an average size of 3.7 nm in the BA matrix (Fig. 2(c)). The corresponding HRTEM image of the CDs@BA3 reveals a crystalline carbon core with a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, consistent with the (002) planes of graphitic-like structures [46]. The AFM image and height analysis of CDs@BA3 (Fig. 2(d)) suggest a roughly spherical shape of CDs with a predominant height of 3–5 nm. By comparison, we found there was a slight variation in the average size of CDs when changing the pyrolysis temperature from 180 °C to 230 °C, 3.0 nm for CDs@BA1, 3.3 nm for CDs@BA2, and 3.5 nm for CDs@BA3, respectively. Therefore, there would be very little contribution of the quantum confinement effect on the pronounced difference in the afterglow luminescence among three CDs@BA samples. Furthermore, the crystallinity of the as-prepared CDs@BA3 was evaluated using X-ray diffraction (XRD). As depicted in Fig. 2(e), the XRD pattern of three CDs@BA3 exhibits two broad peaks around 26° and 41.5°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of the graphite carbon core with the interlayer distances of 0.34 nm and 0.21 nm, respectively. This result is generally consistent with the lattice spacing of 0.34 nm observed in the aforementioned HRTEM image. Similar XRD patterns and peak positions were also observed for CDs@BA1 and.

CDs@BA1 (Fig. S3) indicates a similar crystallization to that of CDs@BA3. The broadness of XRD patterns could be associated with the heteroatomic dopants in the inner lattice or enriched surface oxygen groups [47]. Therefore, the contribution from different crystallinity would be negligible to the significantly distinct afterglow properties among the CDs@BA samples prepared at different temperatures.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of three CDs@BA samples were measured to elucidate the thermally induced effects on their electronic structures and afterglow emission properties. As shown in Fig. 2(f), three CDs@BA exhibit a similar absorption feature in the spectral region of 200–350 nm. The absorption band around 240 nm can be assigned to the π-π∗ transitions of core states, while the band around 340 nm is ascribed to the n-π∗ transitions of the edge band [48]. Compared to CDs@BA1 and CDs@BA2, CDs@BA3 synthesized at a high temperature exhibits a distinct absorption feature with the emergence of two new bands in the visible region. Usually, the π-electron system and surface states can be regulated through surface modification including surface oxidation, leading to the generation or activation of surface state transitions and thus altering the electronic structure and optical properties of CDs [49,50]. The low-energy absorption bands around 400 nm and 520 nm of CDs@BA3 mainly originate from the electronic transitions involving the surface states, which are likely generated by the surface functional groups attached to the edge of the carbon core. This observed variation trend in the absorption spectrum of CDs@BA with changing pyrolysis temperature demonstrates a close correlation with their evolutions in prompt emission color and afterglow color in Fig. 2(a). We further performed FTIR and XPS measurements and analyses on the CDs@BA to characterize their surface states associated with the absorption and PL properties. As presented in Fig. 2(g), the FTIR spectra of three CDs@BA samples exhibit approximate similarity in their characteristic absorption peaks, suggesting a similar conjugated length within their carbon core and amorphous surface structure. The broad absorption of 3200–3400 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the O–H bond and the peak around 1196 cm−1 is ascribed to the C–O/B–O vibrational groups, while the absorption from 1490 cm to 1 to 1715 cm−1 (centered at 1490 cm−1) originate from the combination of carboxylic CO (1650 cm−1), B–O (1490 cm−1) bonds [51]. When increasing the pyrolysis temperature, the progressively increased intensity of the C–O/B–O peak suggests the formation and incremental content of boron oxide polycrystalline in the CDs@BA, accompanied by a gradual decrease in the intensity of the CC bond at 1391 cm−1. The broadening and enhancement of the absorption around 1490 cm−1 to 1650 cm−1 indicate the increased contents and contributions of B–O and CO bonds to the surface states in CDs@BA3. These observations demonstrate a positive correlation between the thermally increased oxidation of CDs@BA and the red-shifted afterglow emission and absorption observed in Fig. 2(a) and (f).

To further investigate the relationship between the afterglow emissions and surface groups of CDs@BA, we analyzed their surface agents and corresponding distributions based on XPS measurements. The full survey scans of XPS spectra of three CDs@BA samples are depicted in Fig. 3 (a–c). The relevant contents of C (285.0 eV), O (531 eV), B (192 eV), and S (165.2 eV) elements were summarized in Table S1. Notably, the oxygen content was increased from 32.5 % to 48.3 %, and the carbon content was decreased with elevating reaction temperatures. These variation trends of oxygen and carbon elements in three CDs@BA demonstrate a close association with the evolution of afterglow emission shown in Fig. 2(a). In other words, the distribution and density of oxygen-containing groups in CDs@BA are crucial to their afterglow emission properties. Additionally, this oxygen doping effect is also conducive to reducing the energy gap and promoting electron accumulation at surface defect states [52], leading to the red-shifted absorption and emission spectra of CDs@BA3. The high-resolution C 1s spectra can be further decomposed into three components as shown in Fig. 3(d), namely CC (285.1 eV), C–O/C–O–B (286.7 eV), and O–CO (289.0 eV) groups, respectively. Interestingly, the quantitative results in Table S2 exhibit a decrease in the relative proportion of CC from 81 % for CDs@BA1 to 69 % for CDs@BA3, as well as an increase of C–O/C–O–B proportion from 12 % for CDs@BA1 to 22 % for CDs@BA3. Concomitantly, the O 1s data display a gradual increase from 27 % to 46 % (Fig. 3(e)–Table S3), according to the changed contents of CO at 532.8 eV and C–O at 534.1 eV. These elemental analyses on O 1s and C 1s spectra demonstrate a good consistency for three CDs@BA samples by increasing oxidation temperature. Likewise, the B 1s spectra (Fig. 3(f) and Fig. S4) can be differentiated into BCO2 at 192.7 eV, B2O3 at 193.8 eV, and B–O at 194.8 eV, suggesting that CDs as emission centers were produced in situ and fixed in the B2O3 matrix through B–C and B–O bonds. The rigid boron oxide framework effectively restricts the non-radiative vibration and rotations of surface groups in these CDs@BA, thereby activating and enhancing their multi-color PL and afterglow emission [53]. Based on the above results and discussions, we can conclude that thermal-induced oxidation regulates the relative distribution and density of different oxygen-containing surface groups, and thus their PL and afterglow emission properties of CDs@BA systems. Schematically, Fig. 3 (g) illustrates the relationship between the oxidation degree and the PL color of CDs@BA. The newly emerged absorption bands around 400 nm and 520 nm are mainly attributed to the electronic transitions of the surface states associated with the oxidized species of CO and C–O groups [54]. The hydroxyl (OH) groups assembled on the inner plane of the carbon core play an important role in the green emission, and the CO and C–O surface groups at the lateral edge are predominantly responsible for the red afterglow emission of CDs@BA [55,56].

The tunable color and broadband PL properties of the CDs@BA samples were meticulously investigated using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopies within nanoseconds and milliseconds time windows. As shown in Fig. 4 (a–c), the emission spectra of the CDs@BA under light excitation display the evolved profiles with increasing oxidation temperature. All the profiles of CDs@BA samples can be decomposed into three distinct emission bands with different proportions, the blue band around 440 nm (P1), the green band around 500 nm (P2), and the red band around 600 nm (P3). In addition to the variations in the proportions of these three emission bands, the tri-emission characteristics and consistent evolution of the PL profiles indicate three distinct radiative processes contributing to the broadband PL properties of CDs@BA samples. As the reaction temperature increases, the intensity ratio of the red band P3 gradually increases from 50 % to 70 %, while the sum fraction of P1 and P2 decreases from 50 % to 30 % (Fig. 4(d)), consistent with the observed color changing in Fig. 2(a) and the content variations in oxygen-containing groups. Additionally, the excitation spectra of CDs@BA indicate that the UV excitation of 300 nm–400 nm mainly contributes to the blue and green emissions, whereas the red band emission can be excited with a broad visible excitation from 400 nm to 500 nm. Combined with the aforementioned results of structural and elemental analyses, the independent PL on excitation wavelength for three CDs@BA (Fig. S5) suggests the broadband emission primarily originated from the distinct emissive surface states. The minimal decrease in PL intensity (Fig. S6) after 30 min of exposure to light underscores their anti-interference characteristics and good photostability in practical applications. To further unravel the distinct broadband PL features and the contributions from different emission centers in CDs@BA1-CDs@BA3, we conducted time-resolved fluorescence measurements on a nanosecond scale and at representative emission wavelengths. As depicted in Fig. 4(e) and (f), the PL dynamics at different detection wavelengths of CDs@BA1 and CDs@BA3 show a biexponential decay feature with a fast component (τ1) and a slow component (τ2). The proportion of long-lived component τ2 increases as the detection wavelength redshifts. Considering the slight contribution from the P1 band, the observed dual lifetimes predominantly originate from the P2 and P3 emissions. The shorter lifetime component τ1 can be attributed to the P2 emissive centers associated with the hydroxyl groups at the lateral edge or planes of CDs, consistent with the usually observed blue and green emission in the hydroxyl-decorated carbon nanomaterials [57]. Accordingly, the longer component τ2 is ascribed to the P3 emission of surface states such as carbonyl, carboxyl, and epoxy groups. The relatively slight contribution from the P1 emission of the core state to the steady-state spectrum and PL decay suggests a dominant non-radiative relaxation of the excited singlet states of CDs@BA. Specifically, the fitting data of PL decay curves at 440 nm and 600 nm for three CDs@BA samples under 355 nm excitation are presented in Table S4. The results manifest that the dynamics at 440 nm are predominantly governed by the edge state transition, with no significant variation in PL lifetime and relative proportion. In contrast, the relative fraction of the shorter component τ1 in the PL decay at 600 nm is markedly decreased from 92 % for CDs@BA1 to 59 % for CDs@BA3, while the weight of the longer lifetime component τ2 is increased from 8 % for CDs@BA1 to 41 % for CDs@BA3. This variation trend of PL dynamics is in good accordance with the observed color evolution from cyan-green of CDs@BA1 to reddish emission of CDs@BA3. Interestingly, as depicted in Fig. 4(b) and Fig. S7, CDs@BA2 exhibits an ultrabroad emission spectrum with the full width at half maximum (FWHM) up to 225 nm and demonstrates a balanced PL with three distinct bands around 440, 530, and 590 nm under UV excitation. The PL spectrum of CDs@BA2 almost covers the entire visible region with the 1931 CIE (x, y) chromaticity coordinated at (0.33, 0.36), closely reaching the ideal value for pure white light (0.33, 0.33) (Inset of Fig. S7). To be expected, this ultrabroad PL property of CDs@BA2 would facilitate its promising potential for white-light display and fabricating advanced electroluminescent devices. Taken together, it could be inferred that the excited electrons of CDs@BA1 predominantly emit photons via the edge state, showing a cyan-green color. Increasing the reaction temperature promotes the generation and activation of the emissive oxygen-containing surface states, thereby leading to the enhancement of the red color emission of CDs@BA3. The rigid polycrystalline boric oxide network effectively stabilizes the excitons and enhances their radiative deactivation by restricting the vibrational relaxation of surface groups in CDs@BA. The synergy of diverse emissive centers with distinct emission spectra and proportions is responsible for the multi-color and broadband PL of CDs@BA under light excitation. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the sample powders, measured using a 355 nm excitation source and an integrating sphere, was approximately 2.8 % (CDs@BA1), 1.9 % (CDs@BA2), and 0.9 % (CDs@BA3).

In addition to the dependence of the PL spectral width and color on the reaction temperatures, the as-synthesized CDs@BA samples also demonstrate time-dependent afterglow properties as shown in Fig. 2(a). As is known, the afterglow emission associated with the triplet state usually originates from the triplet state phosphorescence or the DF via a reverse intersystem crossing from the triplet state. Before analyzing the time-dependent afterglow properties of CDs@BA1 and CDs@BA3, we performed a control experiment to indicate the particularity of DTSA as the precursor for achieving multiple color and time-dependent afterglow emissions. For this purpose, we conducted a control experiment by replacing DTSA with 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (MBA) to synthesize CDs@BA0 with a reaction temperature of 180 °C. According to the molecular structures of DTAS and MBA, these two molecules have almost identical types of elements, and in principle, one DTAS can be separated into two MBAs by breaking the disulfide bond. The time-resolved PL spectra and color changes with time of CDs@BA0, CDs@BA1, and CDs@BA3 are shown in Fig. 5 (a–c). Interestingly, the as-prepared CDs@BA0 exhibits a dominant green color with a PLQYs of 8.2 %, significantly less red emission contribution compared to CDs@BA1 and CDs@BA3, and almost no changing afterglow color with delay time or different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S8). This observation indicates a close correlation between two benzoic acid units in the carbon precursor and the time-dependent color-changing afterglow characteristics of CDs@BA1 and CDs@BA3. Comparably, the dynamic afterglow spectrum and color change over time of CDs@BA0 (Fig. 5(a)) exhibit a persistent fading of dominant green PL with delay time. The corresponding average afterglow lifetime was determined to be 49.5 ms, suggesting a triplet state-related transition process. Similar to the PL evolution under light excitation, the profiles of the afterglow emission spectra of CDs@BA1 and CDs@BA3 also demonstrate the dependence of reaction temperatures, suggesting different contributions from distinct emissive centers to their afterglow colors. Specifically, CDs@BA1 exhibits a green color with a small contribution of red emission in the spectrum upon UV excitation, and a slight color change within the same delay time (Fig. 5(b)). In contrast, CDs@BA3 shows a bright tangerine color under UV light, indicating the significantly increased contribution of red emission (Fig. 5(c)). The afterglow color manifests an obvious evolution from tangerine to green over time, demonstrating a time-dependent feature. Considering the high similarity in their spectral profiles and the very slight Stokes shifts between the PL spectra at zero time delay and the time-dependent afterglow spectra of three CDs@BA samples, we deduce that their afterglow properties predominantly originate from the thermally activated DF of distinct singlet-triplet systems of different emissive centers. Further, we selected two representative detection wavelengths around 490 nm and 600 nm to analyze the afterglow emission dynamics in a subsecond time window for CDs@BA1 and CDs@BA3. The PL decay traces and their corresponding fitting curves are given in Fig. 5(e) and (f), and their fitting parameters are presented in Table S5. The obvious multi-exponential features of the decay curves around 500 nm and 600 nm indicate multiple deactivation channels or emission centers contributing to the afterglow colors. For CDs@BA1, the fast component with an average lifetime of ∼1.2 ms and a large proportion of ∼85 % dominates the decay profiles at 500 nm and 600 nm, while the slow component with a lifetime of ∼12 ms shows a small proportion of ∼15 %. This short-lived green afterglow emission could be attributed to the DF of the edge states, while the long-lived red afterglow to the DF of surface states associated with the oxygen-containing groups [58,59]. Due to the weak afterglow emission from the red emissive centers, CDs@BA1 demonstrates a dominant green afterglow with a very small color change. In contrast, an additional much faster component with a lifetime of ∼0.3 ms is presented in the afterglow decays at 500 nm and 600 nm for CDs@BA3, in addition to the observed two components of ∼1.2 ms and ∼12 ms. The relative fraction of this faster component of ∼0.3 ms is increased from 58 % at 500 nm to 83 % at 600 nm, suggesting an additional red DF center activated by the increased thermally induced oxidation. The enhanced red DF from the activated emissive centers with distinct lifetimes endow the CDs@BA3 with excellent time-dependent afterglow color changing from tangerine to green within 0.5 s, covering a large spectral region of afterglow from 400 nm to 800 nm. In a word, the co-existences of prompt fluorescence and DF from different emissive singlet-triplet states confers three CDs@BA with different broadband PL under light excitations and distinct time-dependent afterglow properties after removing the excitations. The results from the structural analyses and photophysical properties demonstrate a consistent correlation for the as-synthesized CDs@BA at different temperatures. Apparently, more future investigations are needed to precisely identify the involved emissive centers, their specific contributions to the broadband and dynamic afterglow properties and uncover the underlying photophysical mechanisms of these CDs@BA.

The potential applications of the as-synthesized CDs@BA with broadband PL and dynamic afterglow properties were evaluated in the high-level optical information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. As a representative model, Morse code is an archaic form of digital communication that uses a unique combination of dots or dashes to represent various letters or numbers in distinct sequences. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), three CDs@BA samples with distinct colors were filled into a precisely prepared mold to form an “Alphabet Cipher” code array. Information can then be conveyed through color alteration and afterglow persistence. Subsequently, this encoded information can be decoded by correlating with the code array, facilitating the execution of an encryption process. Upon exposure to a 395 nm UV lamp, the pattern emits a code sequence of red (CDs@BA3), orange (CDs@BA2), and green (CDs@BA1) characters. By decoding the code sequence with the designated cipher, we can recognize the letter “W” in the pattern. After turning off the UV excitation, the encoded sequence undergoes a dynamic color transformation, shifting the letter “W” to “A”, “V” and “E” due to the unique dynamic afterglow properties of CDs@BA, and uncovering the encrypted word of “WAVE”. If the code sequence were prepared with the transitional static afterglow materials, the encrypted pattern would exhibit an incorrect message of “WWWW”. Additionally, we further explored the application of CDs@BA in anti-counterfeiting. The elegant “butterfly” (Fig. 6(b)) was prepared using CDs@BA2, and the symbolic Tai Chi (Fig. 6(c)) was constructed utilizing the paired CDs@BA2 and CDs@BA1, while the prestigious emblem of Henan University (Fig. 6(d)) was fabricated using CDs@BA3, respectively. When turning off the UV lamp, the butterfly displayed a captivating color shifting from vibrant yellow to serene green owing to the afterglow emission, compared to a static butterfly under the light excitation. The Tai Chi symbol, composed of CDs@BA2 and CDs@BA1, exhibits a distinct afterglow color-changing in both upper and lower halves. These observations manifest the promising applications of CDs@BA in information storage and encryption with a high level of security protection due to their unique broadband and time-dependent afterglow features.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized several broadband and dynamic afterglow CDs@BAs using a facile thermal treating approach. The multi-color and broadband emissions in the prompt and afterglow PL of CDs@BA are closely associated with the content variations of oxygen-containing groups controlled by the reaction temperatures. The diverse emissive centers associated with the conjugated edge states and oxygen-related surface states demonstrate distinct emission spectra, lifetimes on both nanoseconds and milliseconds scales, and relative proportions. The compromised contributions from different emissive centers confer the CDs@BA with the ultrabroad prompt and afterglow PL spectra that cover almost the entire visible region, manifesting their potential application in the white-light display field. Particularly, the CDs@BA with more oxygen-containing surface groups are endowed with unique dynamic afterglow properties, owing to the additional short-lived DF center and its dominant contribution to the red region emission. Although more investigations are needed to understand the relationship between the types of emissive centers and the photophysical properties of CDs@BA, generating the co-existences of fluorescence and DF from different singlet-triplet states is an effective way to construct broadband and dynamic afterglow luminescent CDs-based materials. Moreover, these as-synthesized CDs@BA with distinct color and dynamic afterglow properties demonstrate promising potentials in optical encryption and time-resolved anti-counterfeiting applications.
